content.full_text:
Re
Fern Por-rrrcAl PnecrrcEs CoMMrssroN
-128 J Street o Sr.rite 620 o Sacranrenro. CA 958 l-l-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
May 26,2009
Brian N. Sipes and Trinka Reynolds
Market Mortgage
567 Sespe Avenue
Fillmore, CA 93015
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-124
Dear Mr. Sipes and Ms. ReynoldS
This letter responds to your request for advice, on behalf of Councilmember
Jamey Brooks, regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").' Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may
have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to
prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair
Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when
it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws
such as common law conflict of interest or Govemment Code Section 1090. We urge
Councilmember Brooks to consult with his city attorney or private counsel on these
provisions of law.
QUESTION
May Councilmember Brooks ask the City of Fillmore to approve the refinancing
of his home and the subordination of the second deed of trust on the home, which was
recorded to secure funds borrowed under a down payment assistance program provided
by the city?
I The PoliticalReform Act is contained in Governmenr Code Secrions 81000 through 91014. All
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-09-124
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
Councilmember Brooks is not making, participating in making, or influencing:i
governmental decision in submitting his request and providing necessary information as
required by the city to process his request so long as he avails himself of the same
procedure typically available to any member of the public and is not be granted special
access to city officials or employees. Assuming Councilmember Brooks or members of
his immediate family wholly own his home, Councilmember Brooks may also appear
before the city council in a public meeting as a member of the public in representation of
himself and his personal interest in his home under Regulation 18702.4(a)(2) and,(b)(l).
Councilmember Brooks may not, however, contact individual officers or employees of
the city in an attempt to influence the decision outside of a public city council meeting.
rACTS
You are requesting advice as the authorized representative of City of Fillmore
Council Member Jamey Brooks. Councilmember Brooks and Lynne Brooks are clients
of your company, Market Mortgage, and purchased their first home October 12, L998, in
the City of Fillmore. When purchasing their home, Mr. and Mrs. Brooks obtained
assistance with their down payment through the City of Fillmore Down Payment
Assistance Program (the "DPAP"). To secure the funds obtained under the DpAp, a
second deed of trust was recorded against the property.
Under the provisions of the DPAP, a borrower may refinance his or her first
mortgage on a property so long as the mortgage payment is decreased by one dollar.
However, in order to refinance, the City of Fillmore must approve the subordination of
the DPAP second deed of trust. Prior to taking office, Councilmember Brooks refinanced
in 2003 with no problems. At that time, the City of Fillmore complied and allowed the
subordination of the DPAP second deed of trust.
Councilmember Brooks was subsequently elected to the City of Fillmore City
Council in 2008. Once again, Councilmember Brooks is attempting to refinance his first
mortgage on the property and will need the City of Fillmore to approve the subordination
of the DPAP second deed of trust. By refinancing his home, Councilmember Brooks will
lower his monthly payment by $300.
A1\ALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, parricipating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial


File No. A-09-124
Page No. 3
interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the govemmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or
more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).)
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a givin govemmentai decision.
(Regulation 1 8700(b)( I )-(8).)
Based upon the facts you have submitted, Councilmember Brooks is a "public
official," within the meaning of the Act.2 (See Sections gzo4g andgz04l.) Additionally,
Councilmember Brooks has an economic interest in his home. As a general rule, when
property is the subject of a govemmental decision, the property is directly involved in the
decision. (Regulation l87}a.2@)(l).) The financial effect of a decision, on real property
directly involved in the decision, is presumed to be material.' (Regulation 18705.b(ai(l))
Accordingly, it appears that Councilmember Brooks has a disqualifying conflict of
interest under the Act that would prevent him from making, participating in making, or
influencing any governmental decision concerning the real property.
Notwithstanding Councilmember Brooks' disqualifying conflict of interest based
upon his economic interest in his home, your question pertains to the limitations placed
upon Councilmember Brooks should he ask the City of Fillmore to approve the
refinancing of his mortgage and the subordination of the DPAP s""ond deed of trust
without participating or taking part in the actual govemmental decision. We, therefore,
must determine whether Councilmember Brooks is making, participating in making, or
influencing a govemmental decision, under the Act, if he appears tifore ttre city council
or contacts city staff regarding the refinancing of his home.
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only when a public official
"make[s], participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use his official position to
influence a govemmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest." (Section 87100; Regulation 18700(b)(2).) In other words, an official
is not prohibited from contacting an agency under the Act's conflict-of-interest
provisions if the official is not making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision. The Commission has adopted a series of regulationr *hich
define "making," "participating in making," and "influencing" a gou"rnmental decision,
and which provide certain exceptions. (Regulations 18702-l87}z.4,copies enclosed.)
2 If a public official's office is listed in Section 872W (*872W filers" include city council
members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, ihen he or she
must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of eionomic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as disiussed in Regulation
18702.5(bX I XB), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) liave the room fbr the
duration ofthe discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions. consent calendars. absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation 1g702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)


File No. A-09-124
Page No.4
Making a Goventmental Decision: A public official "makes a govemmental
decision" when the official, acting within the authority of his or her office or position,
votes on a matter, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course
of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or h"r ug"n"y.
(Section 87100; Regulation 18702. t(a).)
Participating in Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "participates
in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his oi h..
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, ttre official negotiates,
advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker regarding the governmental
decision. (Section 87100; Regulation LB7}Z.Z.)
Influencing a Governmental Decision: There are two rules pertaining to whether
a public official is using or attempting to use his or her official posiiion to inhuence a
govemmental decision. The first rule applies when the govemmental decision is within
or before the public official's own agency or an agency appointed by or subject to the
budgetary control of the public official's agency. (Regulation lgloz.z(a).) In these
cases, if "the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency" then he or rhe is attempting to
influence a govemmental decision. This includes, but is not limited to, "appearhces or
contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer."
The second rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before an
agency other than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of the public official's agency. (Regulation 18702.3ib).) Under
this rule, the official cannot act or purport "to act on behalf of, or as the representative of,
his or her agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an agincy,, to
influence a decision that will have a material financial effect on his or hei economic
interests.
However, an official is not making or participating in making a governmental
decision if he or she makes an appearance "as a member of the geneial public before an
agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function to represent himself or
herself on matters related solely to the official's personal interest . . .." lR"gulation
18702.4(a).) Moreover, an official is not influencing a govemmental decision if the
official "[a]ppears in the same manner as any other member of the general public before
an agency in the course of its prescribed govemmental function solely to represent
himself or herself on a matter which is related to his or her personal interest."
(Regulation I 8702.4(b)( I ).)3
I For Section 87200 filers who must recuse themselves pursuant to Section 87105, Regulation
18702.5(dX3). provides that "[w]hen a personal interest found in [Regulation lB7O2.4(b)] is pre-sent, a
public official may speak as a member of the general public if he or she complies wlttr suLaivisions (b)( I )
and (b)(2) of this regulation, recuses himself or herself from voting on the matter and leaves the dais to
speak from the same area as the members of the public.',


File No. A-09-124
Page No. 5
Regulation 18702.4(b)(l) provides the following in pertinent part:
"An official's 'personal interests' include but are not limited to:
(A) An interest in real property, which is wholly owned by the
official or members of his or her immediate family.
(B) A business entity wholly owned by the official or members of
his or her immediate family.
(C) A business entity over which the official exercises sole
direction and control, or over which the official and his or her
spouse jointly exercise sole discretion and control."
Additionally, an official is not making or participating in making a govemment
decision if the official's actions are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual, or clerical.
(Regulation 18702.4(a)(1).) We have also previously advised that a council member may
contact city staff to "pose general questions which are ministerial in nature" (Torrance
Advice Letter, No. 4-94-084) and that "the Act would not prohibit [a planning
commissionerl from obtaining information otherwise available to the public" (Gallagher
Advice Letter, No. I-94-279).
You have asked, specifically, whether Councilmember Brooks may ask the City
of Fillmore to approve the refinancing of his first mortgage and the subordination of the
DPAP second deed of trust. Presumably, Councilmember Brooks will need to contact
city staff to submit his request for approving the refinancing and to provide necessary
information as required by the city for the processing of the request.
As we have previously determined in the Torrance Advice Letter, supra, and the
Gallagher Advice Letter, supra, contacting city staff to pose general questions that are
ministerial in nature and obtaining information otherwise available to ihe public would
not be considered making, participating in making, or influencing a government decision.
Similarly, Councilmember Brooks is not making, participating in making, or influencing
a governmental decision in submitting his request and providing necessary information as
required by the city to process his request when doing so as a member of itre public.
However, this conclusion is strictly limited to submitting his request and necessary
information required by the city to process the request. In submitting his request,
Councilmember Brooks must avail himself of the same procedure typically ivailable to
any member of the public and must not be granted special access to city officials or
employees.
Assuming Councilmember Brooks or members of his immediate family wholly
own his home, Councilmember Brooks may also appear before the city coun.il in u
public meeting as a member of the public, under Regulation Lg7O2.a@)(2) and(b)(l), in


File No. A-09-124
Page No. 6
representation of himself and his personal interest in his home. Note, however, that
Regulation 187O2.4(a)(2) and (bXl) have been construed naruowly to apply only when an
agency is holding public meetings. Regulation 18702.4(a)(2) and (bxl) do not apply
when an official contacts individual officers or employees of an agency, including city
staff, to participate in making or in an attempt to influence a govemmental decision. isee
Oderman Advice Letter, No. 4-00-082; Gallagher Advice Letter, supra; and,Torrance
Advice Letter, supra.) Accordingly, Councilmember Brooks must also be aware that he
may not contact individual officers or employees of the city in an attempt to influence the
decision outside of a public city council meeting.a
Additionally, while councilmember Brooks may be permitted to make an
appeuuance before the city council under the exception of Regulation lS702.a@)(2) and
(bxl), his comments must be limited to his personal interests, and care should be taken to
clarify that he is not acting in an official capacity. (McHughAdvice Letter, No. I-98-324;
Gallagher Advice Letter, supra; and Larsen Advice Letter, No. 4-87-151.)
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
BGLjgl
Enclosures
a Presumably, most city staff will work for city agencies appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of the city council. However, there is the possibility that some city agencies may not be appointed
by or subject to the budgetary control of the city council. For these agencies, Councilmimber Brooks
would not be attempting to influence an agency's decision if he appears before the agency so long as he
does not act or purport "to act on behalfof, or as the representative of, his or her agency.; lRegulation
18702.3(b).)
